title: YouTube and X Have Become ‘Gateways’ to Nudify Apps
gist: A new ISD report finds mainstream platforms YouTube and X drove the large majority of 5.7 million referral visits to nonconsensual 'nudify' deepfake sites between December 2025 and March 2026, contradicting their own explicit-content policies, as the nudify industry generates an estimated $36 million a year despite new federal and state anti-deepfake laws.

themes.theme: Mainstream platforms are the top traffic source

insights.claim: The Institute for Strategic Dialogue found social networks drove more than 5.7 million visits to nudify sites between December 2025 and March 2026, with YouTube responsible for 1.82 million visits (over 30 percent of referrals) and X second with over 1.3 million visits, meaning mainstream platforms outperform fringe communities like 4chan as referral sources.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Quantifies specific platform-level exposure to content-moderation failure; YouTube and X face the largest direct traffic-referral liability in any regulatory or advertiser scrutiny of nudify-app proliferation.
insights.locator: According to the ISD report, social networks drove

insights.claim: ISD's Melanie Smith says YouTube was not a passive referral source but actively facilitated use of nudify tools through videos reviewing and promoting specific apps and linking to promo codes for free credits.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Strengthens the case that YouTube's enforcement gap is active facilitation rather than mere failure to filter, a meaningfully worse framing for regulatory and legal exposure.
insights.quote: It wasn't just that YouTube was a passive source... In a lot of these cases, it was facilitating the use of these tools as well.
insights.locator: 'It wasn't just that YouTube was a passive source'

themes.theme: Existing platform policies aren't being enforced

insights.claim: YouTube's own policies already prohibit sexually explicit content and links to or advertising for sexually explicit websites, which should logically cover nudify tools and nonconsensual intimate imagery, but ISD found such content 'easily discoverable and accessible' on the platform.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Frames the fix as an enforcement and moderation-resourcing problem rather than a policy gap, useful for assessing whether YouTube's public response will actually change referral volume.
insights.quote: content violating these policies was easily discoverable and accessible on the platform, effectively turning it into a gateway to nudify websites

insights.claim: Some nudify apps and websites charge as little as $1 per generated image, yet a recent WIRED investigation found the industry may generate as much as $36 million in collective annual revenue.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Quantifies the addressable revenue pool sustaining this ecosystem, relevant for assessing payment-processor, app-store, and ad-network complicity and liability exposure and the industry's persistence despite legal risk.
insights.locator: The study also looked at the pricing

insights.claim: ISD found a surprising share of nudify-tool requests are not sexually motivated but aimed at getting targets fired or otherwise compromising their livelihoods, with common targets including current and ex-girlfriends as well as family members such as sisters and cousins.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Broadens the risk model beyond sexual-content moderation into targeted-harassment and reputational-attack risk, relevant to employer HR policy and platform harassment-response frameworks.
insights.quote: A lot of the requests were about getting people fired from jobs and compromising their livelihoods and lives in nefarious ways.
insights.locator: Common targets of nudification apps include

insights.claim: In January 2026, X faced intense backlash after users exploited its Grok AI chatbot to generate nonconsensual nude or sexually suggestive images of women, including minors, prompting X to restrict Grok access to paid users only.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Shows X has direct, recent precedent of hosting an AI tool used for nonconsensual intimate imagery, compounding regulatory and reputational risk alongside its ranking as the second-largest nudify-site referral source.
insights.quote: We remain committed to making X a safe platform for everyone and continue to have zero tolerance for any forms of child sexual exploitation, non-consensual nudity, and unwanted sexual content.
insights.locator: In January 2026, X was subject to intense criticism

insights.claim: The federal Take It Down Act (fully in effect since May 2026) requires platforms to remove nonconsensually distributed images within 48 hours of a victim's takedown request, and Minnesota became the first state to ban nudification apps outright in May 2026, yet nudify apps have 'only continued to spread' and become more accessible.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Legal deterrents so far are outpaced by product growth and accessibility, suggesting continued elevated legal and regulatory-tightening risk ahead for platforms and nudify-app operators alike rather than a resolved issue.
insights.locator: NCII is illegal in the United States

insights.claim: A separate WIRED investigation found reported deepfake cases in more than 90 schools worldwide, showing nudify-app harms are already materializing in institutional K-12 settings, not just among adult users.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Signals a widening liability surface across schools, districts, and ed-tech or parental-control vendors tied to nudify-app proliferation beyond the core social platforms.
